text: Garrett Friedman demonstrates the intracorporeal anastomosis technique for robotic right colectomy. The cut edge of the ileum mesentery is straight, aligning the ileum next to the transverse colon. The third robotic arm provides counter-traction on the bowel to facilitate the enterotomy. The enterotomy is made on the anti-mesenteric side of the bowel, ensuring it is at least two centimeters from the staple line. Cautery is used to enter the lumen, achieving a full-thickness bite of the intestinal wall. The same process is repeated with the transverse colon, using the third arm for counter-traction.

The surgeon ensures the cut edge of the ileum mesentery is straight, aligning the ileum next to the transverse colon. Using the robotic third arm for counter-traction, an enterotomy is made on the anti-mesenteric side of the ileum with cautery, ensuring a full-thickness incision. The same technique is applied to the transverse colon, avoiding the tinea coli. The stapler is inserted into the ileum first, demonstrating its mobility compared to the transverse colon, and achieving proper alignment for anastomosis.

The surgeon opens the colon away from the tinea coli using cautery, similar to the bowel opening technique. Scissors are used with one blade inside and one outside the bowel wall, lifting and cutting. The stapler is inserted into the ilium first, which is more mobile than the transverse colon, allowing easy positioning. The stapler is then brought into the colon, aligning the ilium and colon. After firing the stapler, the third arm retracts the apex of the staple line upwards, revealing a well-aligned, non-bleeding staple line and an exposed crotch of the staple line.

The surgeon fires the stapler, retracting the apex of the staple line with a third arm to align the edges. The staple line is visible, showing no bleeding, and the crotch is widely exposed. Using a V-lock PDS suture on a V-twin needle, the surgeon closes the bottom crotch with full-thickness bites, ensuring the edges of the bowel are well aligned. The apex is retracted posteriorly, exposing the suture line. The surgeon runs the suture from the apex down, taking seromuscular bites to imbricate the suture line, providing extra protection. A thick bite is taken at the staple line's crotch to complete the closure, resulting in a completed anastomosis.

The surgeon closes the bottom crotch of the anastomosis using a V-lock PDS suture on a V-twin needle, ensuring the edges of the bowel are well aligned by retracting the apex of the staple line with a third arm. The suture line is exposed, and the surgeon takes full thickness bites, rolling the last portion of the tip in with a good bite to finish the closure. The suture is run from the apex down, taking seromuscular bites to imbricate the suture line for added protection.

The surgeon uses a long, twelve-inch relock suture to complete the anastomosis, ensuring the edges of the bowel roll in on themselves. Thick, full-thickness bites are taken to secure the closure, particularly at the bottom crotch of the staple line, preventing it from unrolling. The completed anastomosis is visible with the staple line's crotch securely closed.
